meta.title: AI Total Factor Productivity Premium by 2027
meta.question: Will peer-reviewed research or official G7 government statistics confirm that AI-adopting firms demonstrate >=1% higher total factor productivity (TFP) growth than non-adopting peers over any 12-month period between 2025 and 2027?
meta.resolution: Resolved H1 if >=1 peer-reviewed study or official G7 stat release confirms >=2% TFP premium for AI adopters over any 12-month window 2025-2027. H2 if confirmed premium is 0.5-2%. H3 if studies are mixed/inconclusive. H4 if no significant premium confirmed. Resolution date: 2027-12-31.

tagConfig._docs: DATA/BENCHMARK favor confirmed premium. RHETORIC = opinion without data.

H1.label: Strong premium: >=2% TFP differential confirmed in peer-reviewed or government data
H1.unit: % TFP growth differential (AI vs non-AI adopters)
H1.description: A clearly measurable productivity gap emerges in official or peer-reviewed data. AI tools provide sufficient leverage on knowledge work to manifest in aggregate firm-level TFP. Historically significant, comparable to electrification or IT.

H2.label: Marginal premium: 0.5-2% TFP differential confirmed
H2.unit: % TFP growth differential (AI vs non-AI adopters)
H2.description: Real but modest TFP gains, concentrated in specific industries. Visible in firm-level studies but may not show clearly in national accounts. Consistent with early-stage GPT diffusion patterns.

H3.label: Mixed/inconclusive: studies disagree or effect too heterogeneous to confirm
H3.unit: % TFP growth differential (AI vs non-AI adopters)
H3.description: Significant variation by sector, firm size, and adoption quality. Some firms gain substantially; others see no effect. Aggregate washes out or cannot be credibly measured with 2027-available data.

H4.label: No premium: AI redistributes rather than creates aggregate productivity
H4.unit: % TFP growth differential (AI vs non-AI adopters)
H4.description: AI primarily reallocates gains (winner-take-more dynamics) rather than creating surplus. No significant TFP premium when controlling for selection effects. AI is a competitive necessity, not a productivity lever, by 2027.

posteriorHistory.note: Initial priors. Positive premium (H1+H2)=55%. Mixed/null (H3+H4)=45%. Reflects optimism tempered by Solow paradox precedent and 2027 measurement lag.
Sectoral productivity differentials and wage premia suggest real but not-yet-TFP-aggregate premium; supports H2 with nontrivial H1.

redTeam.challenge: No significant shifts proposed; no challenge needed.

posteriorHistory.note: Concentrated gains among leaders with modest aggregate signal support H2 marginal premium band.

redTeam.challenge: No significant shifts proposed; no challenge needed.

posteriorHistory.note: Large cross-industry productivity differentials consistent with >=2% TFP differential when translated to firm-level.

redTeam.challenge: No significant shifts proposed; no challenge needed.

posteriorHistory.note: Modest aggregate TFP and small per-firm AI-patent effect supports H2 (marginal premium 0.5-2%); large premium (H1) less likely; null (H4) weakens.

redTeam.challenge: No significant shifts proposed; no challenge needed.

posteriorHistory.note: Multiple peer-reviewed and central-bank studies converging on >=2% TFP differential at firm and industry level — strong support for H1 (strong premium).

redTeam.challenge: No significant shifts proposed; no challenge needed.

posteriorHistory.note: Goldman's null aggregate finding + Penn Wharton's modest 0.0475 pp 2026 projection cut against a near-term 'strong premium' read; minor shift H2 -> H3.

redTeam.challenge: No significant shifts proposed; no challenge needed.

posteriorHistory.note: Measured TFP contribution in 0.05-0.6 pp/yr range fits H2 marginal premium; not yet H1 strong premium.

redTeam.challenge: No significant shifts proposed; no challenge needed.

tier1_critical.desc: Peer-reviewed study (top-5 economics journal or NBER WP at that venue) reports >=2% TFP premium for AI-adopting firms vs controls over any 12-month window 2025-2027, with credible causal identification.

BLS, ONS, Eurostat, or equivalent G7 statistical agency publishes official productivity analysis confirming a positive TFP differential for AI-adopting firms or sectors with explicit attribution to AI adoption.

tier2_strong.desc: >=3 independent firm-level studies from different research groups find a positive TFP effect of AI adoption, published within a 12-month window.
US BLS multifactor productivity statistics show >=0.5pp acceleration in private business sector MFP growth relative to 2015-2019 trend, with AI adoption cited in official commentary.

>=2 causal firm-level studies (different research groups, peer-reviewed or NBER WP) find labour productivity premium >=3% for AI adopters vs controls, with credible identification. Distinct from TFP — supports productivity-channel hypothesis but does not directly resolve the TFP question.

tier3_suggestive.desc: >=20 S&P 500 companies in a single quarter cite measurable productivity gains from AI tools with specific % improvements on earnings calls. Observable via earnings transcripts.
BLS quarterly labor productivity in information sector exceeds 3% annualized for >=2 consecutive quarters.
>=3 peer-reviewed or credible working-paper studies find a negative TFP effect for AI adopters (worse outcomes than non-adopters), ruling out mixed/inconclusive and confirming active harm.

BLS or G7 statistical agency reports realized 2025 or 2026 private nonfarm TFP growth within 0.5pp of the 2015-2019 trend (no observed inflection above trend), AND no official agency commentary attributes acceleration to AI adoption. Observable on annual TFP releases.

Credible study (PwC, McKinsey, NBER, Atlanta Fed, BIS) reports the top quintile of AI adopters captures >=50% of measured productivity or economic gains, indicating heterogeneous rather than broad-based effects.

anti_indicators.desc: >=3 independent rigorous studies find no statistically significant TFP premium for AI-adopting firms, with well-controlled designs.
Published meta-analysis concludes AI productivity studies are highly heterogeneous with no consensus positive effect, citing methodological concerns.
BLS MFP data for 2025-2026 shows measurable acceleration in information sector AND a published study attributes this to AI adoption with credible causal identification.
>=3 credible institutional projections (CBO, Fed, BLS, Penn Wharton, Goldman, IMF, OECD, BIS) published within a 12-month rolling window converge on AI TFP contribution <=0.6pp annually through 2027.

actorModel.description: Academic economists and statistical agencies are the resolution authorities. AI lab claims are RHETORIC unless backed by independent data.

actor1.name: Key Decision Maker
actor1.role: Primary actor whose decisions drive the outcome

nber_economists.name: NBER / academic economists
nber_economists.role: Produce causal identification studies on AI productivity
nber_economists.decisionStyle: Peer review, replication, long publication lags

methodology: ACTIONS OVER RHETORIC — only verified events move posteriors
TAG EVERYTHING — separate rhetoric from action in evidence log
DON'T FRONT-RUN — set tripwires and let news confirm
SOCIALIZATION DETECTION — recognize narrative prep patterns

evidenceLog.text: LR MIGRATION RESET: posteriors reset to design priors; evidence log preserved.
REPLAY BEGIN: 0 previously-fired indicators will be re-applied chronologically under current LR system. Posteriors reset to design priors; indicator state cleared. Evidence log preserved for audit.
REPLAY COMPLETE: 0 indicators applied, 0 skipped. Final posteriors: {'H1': 0.2, 'H2': 0.35, 'H3': 0.28, 'H4': 0.17}. Indicator firedDates restored to originals for audit.

PwC 2025 Global AI Jobs Barometer: AI skills command 56% wage premium (up from 25%); productivity growth quadrupled in most AI-exposed industries (7% -> 27%, 2018-2024); Hartley et al 2026 finds small positive wage effects with 35.9% US workers using GenAI by Dec 2025; TFP projection +0.09pp in 2027.

GOVERNANCE WARNING on bayesian_update: confidence_inflation, quorum_failure. Reason: Sectoral productivity differentials and wage premia suggest real but not-yet-TFP-aggregate premium; supports H2 with nontrivial H1.

PwC 2026 AI Performance Study (Apr 13): top 20% of firms capture ~75% of AI economic gains. Atlanta Fed/NBER March 2026 survey of ~750 executives: adoption widespread, productivity gains 'measurable but modest, accelerating.' Aghion/Bunel estimate AI +0.25-0.6 pp TFP growth annually over next decade; historical-analog estimates 0.8-1.3 pp.
EPISTEMIC CLAMP: point-estimate posteriors clamped to [0.005, 0.98].
GOVERNANCE WARNING on bayesian_update: confidence_inflation, quorum_failure. Reason: Concentrated gains among leaders with modest aggregate signal support H2 marginal premium band.

PwC 2025 AI Jobs Barometer: productivity growth in AI-exposed industries 27% (vs 7% 2018-2022); AI-skill wage premium 56% (up from 25%); Industries +1 SD AI exposure saw +10% productivity, +3.9% jobs, +4.8% wages.
EPISTEMIC CLAMP: point-estimate posteriors clamped to [0.005, 0.98].
BLS reports private nonfarm TFP +0.8% in 2025 (released March 19, 2026); Chicago Fed (Feb 2026) finds AI-patent firms see 0.3% TFP boost vs 8x lower for standard software patents over 5-year horizon.
EPISTEMIC CLAMP: point-estimate posteriors clamped to [0.005, 0.98].

GOVERNANCE WARNING on bayesian_update: quorum_failure. Reason: Modest aggregate TFP and small per-firm AI-patent effect supports H2 (marginal premium 0.5-2%); large premium (H1) less likely; null (H4) weakens.

Aldasoro et al. (2026) causal study of 12,000+ EU firms: AI adoption raises labour productivity 4% on average, no short-run employment loss. Industries with 1-stdev higher AI exposure saw +10% productivity, +3.9% jobs, +4.8% wages in 2024 (cumulative through early 2026). AI-skilled workers earn 56% wage premium (up from 25% prior year). Hartley et al. 2026 finds small positive wage effects.
evidenceLog.source: Aldasoro et al. via CEPR VoxEU, PwC AI Jobs Barometer 2025/26, Singularity Hub

evidenceLog.text: EPISTEMIC CLAMP: point-estimate posteriors clamped to [0.005, 0.98].

Goldman Sachs (Mar 3, 2026): 'no meaningful relationship between AI and productivity at the economy-wide level' but median 30% productivity gain in two specific use cases. Penn Wharton Budget Model projects AI TFP contribution ~0.0475 pp in 2026, accelerating through decade. Post-ChatGPT TFP averaging 1.11% YoY vs 1.23% historical mean.
EPISTEMIC CLAMP: point-estimate posteriors clamped to [0.005, 0.98].

INDICATOR ADDED: t2_firm_level_labor_productivity_causal (tier2_strong). posteriorEffect=H2 +10pp, H1 +5pp. H3 -10pp, H4 -5pp.. Rationale: Closes a coverage gap revealed by ev_005 (Aldasoro 12k EU firms +4% labour productivity, causal) and ev_003 (PwC +10% productivity in high-AI-exposure industries). Existing tier-2 indicator t2_firm_level_studies_x3 specifies TFP, but the actual empirical literature reports labour productivity. Without this indicator, robust microeconomic evidence triages to NONE..

INDICATOR ADDED: t3_aggregate_tfp_modest_2025_2026 (tier3_suggestive). posteriorEffect=H1 -8pp, H2 +6pp, H3 +1pp, H4 +1pp.. Rationale: Closes a coverage gap revealed by ev_004 (BLS private nonfarm TFP +0.8% in 2025, in line with 2015-2019 trend, no inflection) and ev_006 (Goldman: 'no meaningful relationship between AI and productivity at the economy-wide level'; Penn Wharton +0.0475pp 2026). Currently no indicator captures the *absence* of aggregate TFP acceleration as anti-H1 evidence; this asymmetry is what produces NONE_IMPACT saturation against H1..

INDICATOR ADDED: t3_gains_concentration_heterogeneity (tier3_suggestive). posteriorEffect=H3 +6pp, H1 -3pp, H2 -2pp, H4 -1pp.. Rationale: Closes a coverage gap revealed by ev_002 (PwC 2026: top 20% capture ~75% of AI gains). Concentration is a direct H3 (mixed/heterogeneous) signal; no existing indicator captures distribution of gains, so this evidence dimension was triaging to NONE..

INDICATOR ADDED: anti_h1_institutional_forecasts_modest (anti_indicators). posteriorEffect=H1 -10pp, H2 +6pp, H3 +3pp, H4 +1pp.. Rationale: Closes a coverage gap revealed by ev_002 (Aghion/Bunel 0.25-0.6pp), ev_001 (Penn Wharton +0.09pp 2027), ev_006 (Penn Wharton +0.0475pp 2026, Goldman skeptic). Existing anti-indicators require null findings or meta-analyses; institutional forecast clustering below the H1 threshold is anti-H1 evidence in its own right and currently has no home..

St. Louis Fed Jan 2026 estimates GenAI contribution to TFP growth at ~0.048 pp in 2026 (up from 0.011 pp in 2025). Penn Wharton Budget Model: 1.5% productivity/GDP boost by 2035. PwC: top 20% of firms capture 75% of AI economic gains. Acemoglu maintains <0.66% cumulative TFP gain over 10 years.
evidenceLog.source: St. Louis Fed / Penn Wharton / PwC

evidenceLog.text: EPISTEMIC CLAMP: point-estimate posteriors clamped to [0.005, 0.98].

us_mfp_growth.label: US BLS multifactor productivity growth (private business, % YoY)

dependencies._docs: Upstream: calibration-llm-phd-2027 (higher autonomy -> larger productivity effect on knowledge work).

upstream.reason: G3/G4 research autonomy significantly increases expected productivity gains for knowledge workers.

reviewed_alerts.reason: review_topic_design completed: 4 indicators proposed to close coverage gaps (firm-level labour productivity, aggregate TFP modest, gains concentration, institutional forecast clustering). Ev_001-ev_006 reflect dimensions the prior indicator set did not cover; saturation was a coverage problem, not an evidence problem.

predictionScoring._docs: Calibration infrastructure. Snapshots are auto-created by engine on each posterior update. Record outcome when topic resolves, then compute_calibration_report() for Brier analysis.

conditionalPredictions.prediction: IF strong TFP premium >=2% confirmed, THEN peer-reviewed study published in Nature, Science, or top-tier economics journal (AER, QJE) documents >=2% TFP differential attributable to AI adoption
conditionalPredictions.resolutionCriteria: Observable: Peer-reviewed publication in Nature/Science/AER/QJE/Econometrica by end 2027 measuring >=2% TFP premium with causal identification methodology

AMBER.reasoning: Peer-review timeline is a journal-logistics question, not a regime-change question. Outside AMBER's primary domain.

BLUE.reasoning: Top-tier journal publication cycles are slow (18-24 months); peer review and causal identification for TFP typically lag 2-3 years. 0.67 by EOY 2027 is aggressive given publication timelines.

RED.reasoning: Publication lag in Nature/Science/AER/QJE is 18-36 months. A study documenting 2027 TFP data cannot plausibly be peer-reviewed AND published in these specific journals by Dec 2027. Resolution criteria mechanically infeasible.
RED.modified_suggestion: Extend deadline to 2028-12-31 OR accept NBER working paper as resolution-eligible.

VIOLET.reasoning: Peer-review outcome — outside VIOLET's agency lens but a reasonable observable.

GRAY.reasoning: Narrow venue specification (Nature/Science/AER/QJE/Econometrica) creates hard falsifier.

conditionalPredictions.prediction: IF marginal premium 0.5-2% confirmed, THEN multiple studies (2-3) published by end 2027 documenting 0.5-2% TFP differential with sector-specific variation (finance 1.8%, manufacturing 0.6%)
conditionalPredictions.resolutionCriteria: Observable: >=2 peer-reviewed or government studies published by Dec 2027 report 0.5-2% TFP premium with sectoral heterogeneity documented

AMBER.reasoning: Multiple-study finding with sectoral heterogeneity is well-formed and measurable. 0.70 reasonable.

BLUE.reasoning: Multiple studies with sectoral variation is plausible absorption-consistent pattern. 0.70 reasonable.

VIOLET.reasoning: Multi-study resolution is robust to individual decision-maker preferences.

GRAY.reasoning: Sectoral heterogeneity prediction is a distinct falsifier from aggregate TFP.

conditionalPredictions.prediction: IF H1 (strong premium: ≥2% TFP differential confirmed), THEN factor reallocation follows Schumpeterian but uneven path: capital and labor shift toward AI-adopting firms, driving consolidation of market share; Herfindahl index (sectoral concentration) increases 150-250 points for knowledge-intensive industries (software, professional services, finance) by EOY 2027, indicating structural winner-take-more dynamics.
conditionalPredictions.resolutionCriteria: Observable: Sectoral Herfindahl-Hirschman Index (HHI) for SIC/NAICS software (7372), consulting (8742), and financial services (60) comparing 2027 vs 2025. ≥1 sector shows ≥150pp increase; interpreted as market concentration from AI-driven productivity sorting.

AMBER.reasoning: HHI concentration shift is a clean structural-transformation observable.

BLUE.reasoning: HHI increases 150-250pp from AI sorting assumes winner-take-more overwhelms antitrust/competition response. BLUE expects institutional checks (FTC scrutiny, entry from VC-funded challengers) to moderate concentration. 0.67 too high.

RED.reasoning: Winner-take-more under AI premium is core RED-aligned dynamic.

GRAY.reasoning: HHI is a standard concentration metric; 150pp threshold is meaningful.

conditionalPredictions.prediction: IF H1 (strong premium), THEN measurement paradox emerges: published peer-reviewed TFP estimates claim ≥2% premium, but firm-level variance explodes; adopter-cohort TFP gains are concentrated in top quintile (high-variance, low-persistence) while median firm shows negligible improvement or measurement error noise; macro aggregate masks micro heterogeneity and path-dependent absorptive capacity.
conditionalPredictions.resolutionCriteria: Observable: Two independently published peer-reviewed studies (different author teams, published 2026-2027) measure TFP premium. Study A finds >=2% aggregate premium. Study B finds top-quartile firm gains exceed bottom-quartile by >=4x, with bottom half showing <0.5% TFP improvement. Both use causal identification methodology.

AMBER.reasoning: Measurement paradox / adopter-cohort variance explosion is an excellent AMBER-style regime-change within-aggregate finding.

BLUE.reasoning: Firm-level heterogeneity with aggregate masking is well-documented pattern. Reasonable.

RED.reasoning: Heterogeneity/aggregation fallacy is sharp RED critique — approve.

GRAY.reasoning: Heterogeneity vs aggregate duality is a genuinely distinct test from 279.

conditionalPredictions.prediction: IF H2 (marginal premium: 0.5-2% TFP differential), THEN structural reallocation remains subdued: sectoral Herfindahl index increases <75 points through 2027; firm survival rates between AI-adopter and non-adopter cohorts differ by <1.5 percentage points annually, indicating productivity gains are insufficient to drive Schumpeterian selection.
conditionalPredictions.resolutionCriteria: Observable: (1) Sectoral HHI change 2025-2027 <75pp across knowledge industries. (2) Business Dynamics Statistics (Census) or longitudinal firm data comparing 10-year survival rates: adopter cohort >1.5pp above non-adopter cohort through 2027. <1.5pp difference indicates subdued selection pressure.

BLUE.reasoning: Subdued selection pressure with marginal premium IS BLUE-compatible institutional inertia narrative. 0.71 reasonable.

GRAY.reasoning: Business Dynamics Statistics + HHI dual threshold is falsifiable.

conditionalPredictions.prediction: IF no TFP premium emerges by 2027, THEN BEA NIPA 2027 data shows flat TFP (0-0.5% CAGR 2024-2027) despite AI capex growth >10% CAGR.
conditionalPredictions.resolutionCriteria: BEA National Income and Product Accounts 2027 data (published 2028) show TFP growth 0-0.5% CAGR 2024-2027, despite AI capex growth >10% CAGR.

conditionalPredictions.prediction: IF AI productivity effects are mixed/inconclusive (H3 holds), THEN NBER and academic consensus coalesce around heterogeneous-firm models by Q4 2027, with peer-reviewed meta-analyses showing TFP effects vary more than 5pp by industry, and major central banks incorporate sectoral variation into monetary policy guidance.
conditionalPredictions.resolutionCriteria: Three or more peer-reviewed meta-analyses published in top economics journals; FOMC or ECB monetary policy statements explicitly reference heterogeneous AI adoption trajectories; IMF World Economic Outlook incorporates sectoral TFP divergence forecasts.

criticVerdicts.reasoning: Textbook drift-forward academic-consensus prediction. Economics journals reliably produce heterogeneous-firm models as the synthesizing frame. 64% is reasonable.
Academic consensus coalescing around heterogeneous-firm models is exactly what institutions do when data is ambiguous. Resolution criteria measurable. 0.64 reasonable.
Appropriately humble — bets on academic and central bank institutional behavior, not political actors. FOMC and ECB relatively insulated from electoral pressure. 64% defensible.

Most structurally sound prediction from OCHRE's lens. Academic consensus formation around heterogeneous-firm models reflects natural path-dependence of economic literature. Central bank adoption realistic. 0.64 defensible.
The heterogeneous-firm model convergence prediction is underrepresented in academic/policy literature tracking and provides meaningful contrast to iid 529's corporate fragmentation framing.

conditionalPredictions.prediction: IF evidence accumulates that AI productivity effects are mixed/inconclusive (H3 holds), THEN enterprises abandon unified AI-as-productivity-multiplier frameworks and fragment into sector-specific adoption regimes where AI either delivers clear domain wins or gets relegated to legacy-tool status by Q4 2027.
conditionalPredictions.resolutionCriteria: Major analyst firms (Gartner, IDC, McKinsey) publish fragmentation thesis by Dec 2027; at least 50% of public company earnings calls mention AI deployment heterogeneity or inconsistency rather than broad productivity claims.

criticVerdicts.reasoning: Analyst firms have commercial incentives to maintain broad AI-productivity narratives; 'abandon unified frameworks' overstates shift.
criticVerdicts.modified_suggestion: Reduce to 0.50. Replace 'abandon' with 'publish supplementary sector-specific frameworks alongside existing narratives'.

criticVerdicts.reasoning: Fragmentation of enterprise AI frameworks under inconclusive evidence is exactly the institutional response RED anticipates. 0.65 defensible.
Prediction about named corporate actors — Gartner, IDC, McKinsey analysts and Fortune 500 CEOs. Fragmentation consistent with how CEOs respond to inconclusive evidence. 65% defensible.

Enterprise adoption patterns follow diffusion curves with sector-specific absorption rates — heterogeneity is the default outcome of large-N adoption, not uniformity. Fragmentation into domain-specific regimes is exactly the emergent outcome OCHRE expects. 0.65 appropriate.
Distinct from iid 517 despite sharing H3 condition — 529 tracks corporate regime fragmentation while 517 tracks academic consensus formation.

conditionalPredictions.prediction: IF AI productivity effects remain inconclusive (H3), THEN labor-market debate shifts from AI displaces jobs to AI forces skill-tier bifurcation, triggering regime change in education policy where mid-career credential programs accelerate 4x by end 2027.
conditionalPredictions.resolutionCriteria: US vocational/certificate enrollment growth accelerates to more than 15% YoY (vs. historical 2-4%); at least 3 Fortune 500 companies establish internal AI-skills bootcamps with 10k or more employee throughput by end 2027.

criticVerdicts.reasoning: 4x acceleration in mid-career credential programs too fast; capacity constraints, accreditation timelines brake acceleration.
criticVerdicts.modified_suggestion: Reduce to 0.42. Replace 4x acceleration with 10-15% YoY growth in certificate enrollment.

criticVerdicts.reasoning: Educational institutions are slow-moving bureaucracies that cannot scale programs 4x on 2-year timeline. Bottleneck is instructors, curriculum approval, accreditation — not student demand.
criticVerdicts.modified_suggestion: Lower to 0.44. Separate credential enrollment criterion from corporate bootcamp criterion. Add supply constraint flag.

criticVerdicts.reasoning: 4x acceleration requires named actors (congressional appropriators, DOL administrators, community college boards) to fund and mandate this. Under current fiscal and political conditions (Trump DOE, congressional austerity), federal funding is being cut, not expanded.
criticVerdicts.modified_suggestion: Lower to 0.36. Resolution should require specific federal appropriation or mandate.

criticVerdicts.reasoning: Education institutions cannot scale supply 4x within 2-3 years: accreditation cycles, faculty capacity, capital investment operate on 5-10 year horizons. 15% YoY growth plausible for unaccredited bootcamps but not formal credentialing.
criticVerdicts.modified_suggestion: Reduce to 0.44. Enrollment growth in non-accredited AI skills programs accelerates >15% YoY but formal credential infrastructure grows only 5-8% YoY due to institutional inertia.

criticVerdicts.reasoning: '4x acceleration' is large, underspecified multiplier. 4x relative to what baseline, over what period? The 10k-employee bootcamp bar is reasonable but disconnected from the headline '4x' claim.
criticVerdicts.modified_suggestion: Replace '4x acceleration' with: US certificate/credential enrollment growth exceeds 20% YoY for two consecutive years (2026-2027), versus historical 2-4% baseline.

conditionalPredictions.prediction: IF productivity evidence stays mixed (H3 holds), THEN venture capital funding for AI infrastructure shifts from growth-stage to acquisition-as-exit mode, signaling regime change from AI as new platform to AI as embedded component by Q2 2027.
conditionalPredictions.resolutionCriteria: VC funding to AI infrastructure startups drops more than 40% YoY; M&A activity for sub-500M USD AI companies increases more than 2x; median time-to-exit for AI unicorns falls from 8 to 4 years by Q2 2027.

criticVerdicts.reasoning: 40%+ YoY drop in AI infrastructure funding by Q2 2027 runs against LP multi-year commitments and expanding addressable market.
criticVerdicts.modified_suggestion: Reduce to 0.40. Replace 40% VC funding drop with 'funding growth rate slows below 10% YoY'.

criticVerdicts.reasoning: VC markets are not efficient processors of productivity data — they chase narrative momentum. FOMO among LPs is structural. 40% VC funding drop in 12 months is extremely aggressive given capital commitment lag.
criticVerdicts.modified_suggestion: Lower to 0.38. Reframe: bifurcation — mega-deals accelerate while Series A/B dries up. Extend to Q4 2027.

criticVerdicts.reasoning: VC funding dynamics driven by named GPs and LP return expectations — concrete actor incentives that respond to evidence of inconclusive productivity gains. 62% reasonable.

VC funding dynamics sensitive to interest rate cycles and LP risk appetite. LP commitments are multi-year and GPs are structurally incentivized to deploy committed capital. Median time-to-exit compression requires secondary market liquidity conditions that are the binding constraint.
criticVerdicts.modified_suggestion: Reduce to 0.45. M&A activity increase more likely than funding compression at this timeline. Soften funding decline threshold to >20% YoY.

criticVerdicts.reasoning: VC funding shift from growth-stage to acquisition-as-exit is a distinct leading indicator not tracked elsewhere. 40% YoY funding drop and 2x M&A increase independently verifiable from Pitchbook/CB Insights.

criticVerdicts.modified_suggestion: Lower to 0.44. Separate credential enrollment criterion from corporate bootcamp criterion. Add supply constraint flag.

criticVerdicts.reasoning: 4x acceleration requires named actors (congressional appropriators, DOL administrators, community college boards) to fund and mandate this. Under current fiscal and political conditions (Trump DOE, congressional austerity), federal funding is being cut, not expanded.
criticVerdicts.modified_suggestion: Lower to 0.36. Resolution should require specific federal appropriation or mandate.

criticVerdicts.reasoning: Education institutions cannot scale supply 4x within 2-3 years: accreditation cycles, faculty capacity, capital investment operate on 5-10 year horizons. 15% YoY growth plausible for unaccredited bootcamps but not formal credentialing.
criticVerdicts.modified_suggestion: Reduce to 0.44. Enrollment growth in non-accredited AI skills programs accelerates >15% YoY but formal credential infrastructure grows only 5-8% YoY due to institutional inertia.

criticVerdicts.reasoning: '4x acceleration' is large, underspecified multiplier. 4x relative to what baseline, over what period? The 10k-employee bootcamp bar is reasonable but disconnected from the headline '4x' claim.
criticVerdicts.modified_suggestion: Replace '4x acceleration' with: US certificate/credential enrollment growth exceeds 20% YoY for two consecutive years (2026-2027), versus historical 2-4% baseline.

conditionalPredictions.prediction: IF productivity evidence stays mixed (H3 holds), THEN venture capital funding for AI infrastructure shifts from growth-stage to acquisition-as-exit mode, signaling regime change from AI as new platform to AI as embedded component by Q2 2027.
conditionalPredictions.resolutionCriteria: VC funding to AI infrastructure startups drops more than 40% YoY; M&A activity for sub-500M USD AI companies increases more than 2x; median time-to-exit for AI unicorns falls from 8 to 4 years by Q2 2027.

criticVerdicts.reasoning: 40%+ YoY drop in AI infrastructure funding by Q2 2027 runs against LP multi-year commitments and expanding addressable market.
criticVerdicts.modified_suggestion: Reduce to 0.40. Replace 40% VC funding drop with 'funding growth rate slows below 10% YoY'.

criticVerdicts.reasoning: VC markets are not efficient processors of productivity data — they chase narrative momentum. FOMO among LPs is structural. 40% VC funding drop in 12 months is extremely aggressive given capital commitment lag.
criticVerdicts.modified_suggestion: Lower to 0.38. Reframe: bifurcation — mega-deals accelerate while Series A/B dries up. Extend to Q4 2027.

criticVerdicts.reasoning: VC funding dynamics driven by named GPs and LP return expectations — concrete actor incentives that respond to evidence of inconclusive productivity gains. 62% reasonable.

VC funding dynamics sensitive to interest rate cycles and LP risk appetite. LP commitments are multi-year and GPs are structurally incentivized to deploy committed capital. Median time-to-exit compression requires secondary market liquidity conditions that are the binding constraint.
criticVerdicts.modified_suggestion: Reduce to 0.45. M&A activity increase more likely than funding compression at this timeline. Soften funding decline threshold to >20% YoY.

criticVerdicts.reasoning: VC funding shift from growth-stage to acquisition-as-exit is a distinct leading indicator not tracked elsewhere. 40% YoY funding drop and 2x M&A increase independently verifiable from Pitchbook/CB Insights.
